speaker: interviewer
text: October the 16th, 1996. The survivor is Hilda Pierce. Maureen Halpert, interviewer. It's in Laguna Niguel, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Maureen, that's M-A-U-R-E-E-N, Halpert, H-A-L, P as in Paul, E-R-T. I'm interviewing today Hilda Pierce. It's October 16th, 1996. We are in Laguna Niguel. The language is English. Can you give me your full name and spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilda Pierce, H-I-L-D-A, P-I-E-R-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me your name at birth, and spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Hilda Hertha Harmel, H-I-L-D-A, second name H-E-R-T-H-A, last name H-A-R-M-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you used any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My first married name was Rubin. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-B-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- have you used any nicknames at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband calls me Heidi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-I-D-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Esther. E-S-T-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and country were you born, and spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, Austria, V-I-E-N-N-A. In German, it's spelled W-I-E-N. And Austria, A-U-S-T-R-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 8th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And tell me about your parents. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Eveline and my father's name, Julius Harmel, H-A-R-M-E-L. And my mother was born in a little town outside of Vienna, but my father was born in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother born, what town? It's called Lassee, L-A-S-S-E-E. Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an art appraiser, an antique dealer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was his profession after I was born. Before that, he worked for the Austrian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- actually, he worked for a traffic direction-- direction. He directed traffic of trains all over Austria. A traffic manager I guess we'd call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Did she work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two stores. My father had an antique store. And my mother had a textile store, like a fabric shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have people working for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had employees, both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Two, three each. And that changed over the years. I really was raised by maids and cooks, household help, because both my parents were always working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an apartment in a big apartment building that was sort of a court arrangement. The building surrounded a large courtyard, where all the children that lived in the building played. And it was in the 4th District of the Vienna. Vienna was divided into districts, and the 4th District was called the Diplomatic Center, so all the various consulates were in that district. And we lived almost at the edge of a palace called the Prince Eugene Belvedere, which had a beautiful garden and park, where we used to go and play.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the inside of your home like? Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was like a two-bedroom apartment. And the only thing that was different was the heating method. We had what was called a [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a big stove built with tiles. And it looked like a castle sitting in the corner. And everything else was just like an American apartment, except the building was old. It was built, I would imagine, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the 1900s, early 1900s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I had two sets of grandparents, except my paternal grandparents died when I was one and three years old. My grandfather was in the same business as my father. And my grandmother was a housewife, because my father was one of 11 children, and so she was busy. Of course, they didn't all survive. And my maternal grandparents lived in the small town, Lassee that I mentioned before, where my grandfather was what you would call a gentleman farmer. He owned a lot of land and it was farmed. And during the First World War, my mother was in charge of 80 Russian prisoners of war that worked on the land, because all the Austrian men had to go to war, so they-- they had these prisoners of war come to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father in the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my mother's brother and my father's brothers, and-- well, everybody in the family, because they took everybody. And of course, Austria lost the war, was chopped up. And from a fairly large country of 64 million population, it then became a very tiny country with just this over-large big city of Vienna with this tiny little country behind it of only 6 million total. And there was great starvation, because there was no food in the country. It was a mountainous country, and they couldn't grow any food. The only place that was flat where they had food was where my grandfather had the farm, which was right along the Danube going toward the Czech border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any starvation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- we-- at home, we were very well-off, because my mother and I took the train to the farm every week and brought home suitcases full of food. But my mother and father took me to the outskirts of Vienna to show me where people were living in holes dug in the ground, and with just cardboard to cover up the hole they were so poor and starving. And the veterans came back without limbs, sitting on the street begging in tattered uniforms. They couldn't be taken care of. I mean, it was terrible. And we-- I used to be allowed to invite children home for dinner, because we had meat and they never saw meat. So-- and even I, although I was well fed, had rickets as a child, because we didn't have any fresh fruit in the winter. So there was great starvation after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, Moses Aron Harmel, and my grandmother, Rosa Harmel. And my other grandparents, were Theresa or 'Tuh-ray-zay' Kohn and my grandfather Adolf Kohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about religious life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, both sides, both my grandparents had kosher households. My father was a non-practicing Jew. And I had no religious life at home, except that we were invited to seders at my aunt's house every year. And I had a religious instruction at school. Austria being a Catholic country was very observant of religious beliefs. And a Catholic priest came to class every day and taught catechism. And I was the only Jewish child in the school, so I was allowed to stay in the room and just had to sit-in the back row. And I was a very bright child and I knew all the answers. And I was forever raising my arm and wanted to answer the question. And the priest always said, you keep quiet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have seders at home or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went to my relatives for seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm also a non-practicing Jew, but very aware of the fact that I'm Jewish and very interested in Jewish causes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. That's the only--

speaker: interviewer
text: You speak any other?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school we had French. I-- I had French, Italian, and English at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like between you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely close. Extremely close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things would you do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every Sunday when the stores were not open, we would go to museums in the winter. Every single Sunday my father took me to the various museum. And all of Vienna was like a museum, because the Baroque architecture and the churches and the buildings were very interesting. And in the summer, we'd go hiking in the Vienna woods and have picnics and have a rucksack, which is a backpack, and eat wienerschnitzel and gurkensalat, which is veal, breaded veal cutlet and cucumber salad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city of Vienna like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was not as beautiful as it is now because it was all black and sooty and sort of dismal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was after the war, so there was that much poverty. People couldn't afford to paint their houses or do anything on the streets. And it was dismal. It was also torn by so much political strife. And from the time I was six years old, I was very interested in politics-- not only me. All the other children were too, because we were practicing with gas masks in-- in first grade. And we learned how to put out incendiary bombs. There were revolutions and putsches and civil war and all kinds of strife, so it was a very interesting childhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Hitler came in. I mean, we had so many-- we had a chancellor assassinated in the parliament. That was Dollfuss, the very short chancellor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I know is that he was assassinated by someone who disguised himself as a cleaning person and assassinated him. And then his heir was a young man who used to be minister of education, by the name of Kurt von Schuschnigg, whom I subsequently met at a cocktail party in Highland Park, Illinois. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Schuschnigg was head of-- Austria was a republic after the war. And it was a Social Democratic republic. It's not Socialist in the sense of Socialist here. It was Social Democratic, which was milder. It was to the left, but not as far left as a Socialist. After Dollfuss was assassinated [SIGHS] and Schuschnigg came in, it-- Austria became Christian Socialists, which was the religious right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe '35, '36. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father, who held many honorary positions-- he had a card printed at one time where he was president or board member of 26 different organizations, including his professional organization, the dealers of antiques. And when Schuschnigg came in, he got one letter after the other kicking him out of all his jobs because he was Jewish. And the rumblings of National Socialism came long before the Anschluss. I would say starting in 1935 I noticed that among my friends there was whispering. They were wearing white stockings and blue cornflowers, which was the sign of National Socialism. And you could tell. There were all kinds of underground movements. And it-- it was clear to me as a child of 14 or 13 that it wasn't going to be long before Hitler would get into Austria. But my father was in total denial.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back a little bit and find out firstly-- so you said you went to a synagogue. Do you recall the name of it or the rabbi or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a synagogue in the 10th district and it was called the [INAUDIBLE] Temple. And I think the name of the rabbi was Mandel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was school like? Tell me me about school, the relationship with other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grammar school was very close to our house and it was an easy walk. I was very happy at school. There were no other Jewish children in that-- in my class at the time. And-- but grammar school was only four years. After four years, you went either to a public high school, which was four years, or to a semi-public high school, which was then eight years. And that was called gymnasium. And for that, the parents had to pay, very little but they had to pay. And I went to what was called a realgymnasium, which specialized in mathematics, geometry, and was really for engineers, which was not my forte particularly, because I was much more artistic than mathematically inclined. But I went there. And it was a long streetcar ride, three streetcars. At the age of 10, I had to go into what was then considered bad traffic and changed streetcars three times to go to school. That's how far away it was. There were two other Jewish girls in my class. And the professors were very strict and there was a lot of homework. And I think I enjoyed it. I don't really remember that I was unhappy. I was a very good student and sort of a goody-goody. And I was very strictly brought up. And my parents insisted that I excel in school. Because I was weak in math, I had a tutor at the age of 10 in math in order to get top grades, because they insisted that I study hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the other students treat you that weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well at first, until the political situation became such that they took sides. And we would go to major rallies in Vienna, let's say on the 1st of May, which was the workers' Labor Day. And instead of singing the Austrian anthem, they would [SIGHS] put the words of the German anthem in, because it was the same tune. The music was the same of the German anthem and the Austrian, so they exchanged a word. Instead of singing about Austria, they sang about Deutschland, Deutschland, uber alles. And then they took sides. And there was whispering. And I was discriminated against in games and in sports. I was very good at certain sports and they wouldn't pick me as a captain or whatever. And it was very apparent that there was a lot of discrimination.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this start?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when I was about 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- what year was this, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was '33, '34, when Hitler first came into Germany. And this was the underground movement. They didn't really expect any re-unification of the Third Reich at that time yet. But they were inclined to be very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always wanted to be an artist. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was the only goals I had. And I-- I was very interested in children's groups that my parents put me into to fight against ever having another war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What groups--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sang songs about never having war again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What groups were they, and how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in different groups, summer camps, and always through the Social Democratic Party. My father felt it was important that I mingle with the worker's children, rather than just the elite. And [SIGHS] those were the groups and we were indoctrinated that war is a terrible thing and that we should fight against it, and let the big shots fight among themselves and not involve the people, because we suffered so much in the war. And we were taught that the Versailles Treaty was very unfair, which of course history has proven so. And we were all extremely politically aware, all the children, like I said, from age 6 on. We didn't know football scores. But we knew about politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your friends like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had close friends in my building where I lived. We had a group of children that played in this courtyard. And all but two became ardent Nazis. And the terrible thing was when Hitler marched into Austria, they brought out their uniforms. They had uniforms. And these young boys and girls would spit on me. They were my best friends, and wouldn't talk to me and wouldn't-- like I said, spit at me and throw stones and it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what could you do? First you tried to talk to them. And then your parents tell you that if you talk back, you're going to get killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't talk back. You just evade them or you avoid them and walk past fast. And that's all you can do. But it-- it was so pervasive and it-- nobody can really imagine what it is like when the national policy becomes-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it becomes stylish to be nasty to your old friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- they want to-- in the-- in the parks, there were benches where you couldn't sit. On the street, you couldn't take certain forms of transportation. And it goes right to the very center of your being.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw it even before Hitler marched into Austria, long before. I would go to a movie with some friends and you'd see hordes of young people marching, singing the Horst Wessel song, you know, when Jewish blood spurts from knives. And this was going on for at least two years before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you try doing as a child in your spare time, or growing up, what were your hobbies.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no spare time. We had no spare time. We had school. We had after-school activities, dance classes, ice skating classes, horseback riding classes. There was no spare time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what spare time there was, I would go to the various stores that my parents had and do my homework.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that you saw that affected you for the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just told you. This was years. And my aunt and uncle, my father's sister, and their son left in 1934 for what was then Palestine. They saw it coming. They tried to get their money out. They lost 2/3 of their fortune by doing that, because it was not legal to take any money out of Austria. And I begged my father to do that too and he wouldn't. He called my uncle the meshugganah Max, you know, the crazy Max, because he did that and lost so much money. And then my aunt came to visit from Palestine, crying bitterly how she hated it there and how hard it was and how terrible it was. And my cousin didn't like it. And she didn't want to go back. My father forcively had to push her back on the train to take the ship back to Israel, which was then Palestine. They lived in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What where their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name was Fellman, and my aunt Adele and my Uncle Max and my cousin Fritz. And they're all dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw him march into Vienna. And I was on the Ringstrasse, which is the main thoroughfare in Vienna. It rings the inner city. And I saw thousands of people fall to their knees and greeting him with flowers, with kisses. And there seemed to be no ill will. It-- he was welcomed with open arms, although Schuschnigg made a speech as Hitler was crossing the border. But it was all arranged beforehand, because Schuschnigg the chancellor had gone to Berchtesgarden. There was a meeting there and Schuschnigg had to accept a Nazi into his government in Vienna. His name was Seyss-Inquart. And that was sort of a quisling. And it was all arranged. The Anschluss was all arranged beforehand. Schuschnigg was very weak. And he couldn't resist, because he didn't expect or get any help from the-- from England or France. We had hoped that in the last minute somebody would come to our aid, but nobody did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affected me that we got thrown out of school, that the people that owed my father money in the stores, nobody paid. Everybody took merchandise, bought it, and never paid. We desperately tried to get out. I tried to get out. A couple of my friends and I spent every single day going from one consulate to another to try to find a way of getting out or-- and find out the regulations that you had to follow to get a visa to another country. We found out that you couldn't come to America, which was my first choice, because I thought that Broadway was paved with gold and there was a dollar print waiting for me at every corner, and I was looking for that. And my parents were going to go to Israel to join my father's sister. And I also had an uncle in Alexandria, Egypt. And they tried to get my parents to get to Israel. But in order to do that, they had to go to Cyprus first. So my aunt from Israel and my uncle from Alexandria went to Cyprus to arrange for my parents to go to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop right here. We're going to be going on to the next tape. Thank you.
